source.definition: An adjective describing someone who feels or shows jealousy, typically fear of losing something valuable, often a partner's love.

explanation.sourceText: The adjective "jealous" derives from the noun "jealousy," which originally combined ideas of zeal and intense desire or suspicion.
explanation.targetText: Cuvântul englezesc „jealous” derivă din substantivul „jealousy”, care inițial combina ideile de zel și dorință intensă sau suspiciune.

target.definition: Care simte sau arată gelozie, adică teamă de a pierde ceva valoros, de obicei dragostea partenerului.

explanation.sourceText: The Romanian adjective „gelos” was borrowed from the Italian „geloso”, which comes from the Latin „zelosus” (jealous).
explanation.targetText: Adjectivul românesc „gelos” a fost împrumutat din italianul „geloso”, derivat din latinul „zelosus” (gelos).

examples.sourceText: He is very jealous of his girlfriend.
examples.targetText: El este foarte gelos pe prietena lui.

examples.sourceText: She became jealous when he talked to others.
examples.targetText: Ea a devenit geloasă când el vorbea cu alții.
Oamenii geloși se simt adesea nesiguri.

learningTips.text: The English adjective ends with -ous, while the Romanian equivalent often ends with -os, e.g., jealous – gelos.
Remember that "gelos" sounds like the Italian "geloso," helping you link it to the meaning jealous.
The Romanian word comes from Latin "zelosus," meaning jealous, so you can connect the roots easily.
Imagine someone turning 'yellow with jealousy' to recall the Romanian word "gelos."
